section.title: Gethsemane and the Arrest
section.sectionRationale: Jesus foretells that all will fall away and Peter will deny Him. In Gethsemane He wrestles in agony with the cup set before Him, submitting fully to the Father's will, then is betrayed with a kiss, refuses rescue, and is arrested as the disciples flee.
section.thesis: Facing the cup of God's judgment, Jesus is overwhelmed with sorrow yet surrenders wholly to the Father's will, and He goes to His betrayal and arrest not as a victim but as the willing Son who fulfills the Scriptures and could summon legions of angels but will not.
section.frameNote: Three beats: the prediction that all will fall away and Peter will deny; the agony and threefold prayer in Gethsemane; and the betrayal, the sword, and the arrest as the disciples flee.

verses.text: Then Jesus said to them, “This very night you will all fall away on account of Me. For it is written: ‘I will strike the Shepherd, and the sheep of the flock will be scattered.’
But after I have risen, I will go ahead of you into Galilee.”
Peter said to Him, “Even if all fall away on account of You, I never will.”
“Truly I tell you,” Jesus declared, “this very night, before the rooster crows, you will deny Me three times.”
Peter replied, “Even if I have to die with You, I will never deny You.” And all the other disciples said the same thing.

units.label: Gethsemane
units.note: not as I will, but as You will

verses.text: Then Jesus went with His disciples to a place called Gethsemane, and He told them, “Sit here while I go over there and pray.”
He took with Him Peter and the two sons of Zebedee and began to be sorrowful and deeply distressed.
Then He said to them, “My soul is consumed with sorrow to the point of death. Stay here and keep watch with Me.”
Going a little farther, He fell facedown and prayed, “My Father, if it is possible, let this {{poterion|cup}} pass from Me. Yet not as I will, but as You will.”
Then Jesus returned to the disciples and found them sleeping. “Were you not able to keep watch with Me for one hour?” He asked Peter.
“{{gregoreo|Watch}} and pray so that you will not enter into temptation. For the spirit is willing, but the body is weak.”
A second time He went away and prayed, “My Father, if this cup cannot pass unless I drink it, may Your {{thelema|will}} be done.”
And again Jesus returned and found them sleeping, for their eyes were heavy.
So He left them and went away once more and prayed a third time, saying the same thing.
Then He returned to the disciples and said, “Are you still sleeping and resting? Look, the hour is near, and the Son of Man is betrayed into the hands of sinners.
Rise, let us go! See, My betrayer is approaching!”

units.label: The betrayal and arrest
units.note: a kiss, a sword, and the Scriptures fulfilled

verses.text: While Jesus was still speaking, Judas, one of the Twelve, arrived, accompanied by a large crowd armed with swords and clubs, sent from the chief priests and elders of the people.
Now the betrayer had arranged a signal with them: “The One I kiss is the man; arrest Him.”
Going directly to Jesus, he said, “Greetings, Rabbi!” and kissed Him.
“Friend,” Jesus replied, “do what you came for.” Then the men stepped forward, seized Jesus, and arrested Him.
At this, one of Jesus’ companions drew his sword and struck the servant of the high priest, cutting off his ear.
“Put your sword back in its place,” Jesus said to him. “For all who draw the sword will die by the sword.
Are you not aware that I can call on My Father, and He will at once put at My disposal more than twelve legions of angels?
But how then would the Scriptures be fulfilled that say it must happen this way?”
At that time Jesus said to the crowd, “Have you come out with swords and clubs to arrest Me as you would an outlaw? Every day I sat teaching in the temple courts, and you did not arrest Me.
But this has all happened so that the writings of the prophets would be fulfilled.” Then all the disciples deserted Him and fled.

poterion.gloss: A cup. In the prophets the cup is the cup of God's wrath and judgment that the wicked must drink; in Gethsemane Jesus faces this cup, the judgment due to sinners, and prays that it might pass, yet resolves to drink it in the sinner's place.

gregoreo.gloss: To stay awake, be alert, keep watch. Jesus asks His friends to watch with Him and to watch and pray against temptation; their failure to stay awake exposes the weakness of the flesh even when the spirit is willing.

thelema.gloss: Will, what one desires or purposes. The center of Gethsemane is the surrender of Jesus' human will to the Father's: not as I will, but as You will, may Your will be done, the obedience by which He embraces the cross.

basic.context: On the way to the Mount of Olives, Jesus foretells that all the disciples will desert Him that night, quoting Zechariah about the struck Shepherd, and He warns Peter he will deny Him three times. In Gethsemane He is overwhelmed with sorrow and prays three times that the cup might pass, yet each time surrenders to the Father's will while His friends sleep. Judas arrives with an armed crowd and betrays Him with a kiss. A companion draws a sword, but Jesus stops him, refuses angelic rescue so the Scriptures may be fulfilled, and is arrested as all the disciples flee.
basic.hermeneutics: Gethsemane shows the genuine humanity of Jesus: real anguish, real prayer, real submission. The cup is not merely death but the judgment of God against sin. His refusal to summon angels shows the cross is chosen, not forced. The disciples' failure and flight fulfill His words and expose the weakness that only His faithfulness will redeem.
basic.originalLanguages: Jesus faces the poterion, the cup of God's judgment, and prays it might pass. He asks His friends to gregoreo, to keep watch, but they sleep. And the heart of His prayer is thelema, the Father's will: not as I will, but as You will.
basic.typology: The struck Shepherd and scattered sheep fulfill Zechariah 13. The cup of wrath fulfills the prophetic image of the cup God's judgment pours out. Jesus, the obedient Son in a garden, reverses Adam's disobedience in Eden.
basic.christ: Jesus is the Shepherd struck for the sheep, the obedient Son who drinks the cup of judgment in our place, and the willing sacrifice who could call twelve legions of angels but lays down His life to fulfill the Scriptures.
basic.god: The Father's will is the cross, and the Son submits to it in love. God ordains the suffering foretold by the prophets, and He is powerful to rescue yet purposes salvation through the surrender of His Son.
basic.crossReferences: Zechariah 13 gives the struck Shepherd. Isaiah 51 and Jeremiah 25 give the cup of God's wrath. Hebrews 5 recalls Jesus' loud cries and prayers. Philippians 2 shows His obedience unto death.
basic.oneStory: In a garden, where the first man grasped his own will and fell, the last Adam surrenders His will to the Father and takes up the cup that fell should have poured on us. Struck as the Shepherd so the scattered sheep might be gathered, Jesus goes willingly to His arrest, fulfilling the Scriptures. The story moves toward the cross where the cup is drunk to the dregs, and the obedience of Gethsemane secures the salvation Eden lost.

context.literary: This unit bridges the Supper and the trials, moving from prediction to fulfillment: Jesus foretells desertion and denial, then the narrative shows Gethsemane, the arrest, and the flight. Matthew highlights Jesus' sovereignty even in weakness, the threefold prayer paralleling Peter's coming threefold denial, and the repeated refrain that all this fulfills the Scriptures.
context.historical: Gethsemane, meaning oil press, was an olive grove on the Mount of Olives, a customary retreat for Jesus. A cohort or temple guard with swords and clubs came by night to avoid the crowds. A kiss was a normal greeting between disciple and rabbi, making its use for betrayal especially treacherous. A Roman legion numbered around six thousand, so twelve legions was an overwhelming heavenly force.
context.canonical: The struck Shepherd quotes Zechariah 13:7. The cup draws on Isaiah 51:17, Jeremiah 25:15, and Psalm 75:8, the cup of God's wrath. Gethsemane's obedience answers Adam's fall in the garden, Genesis 3, and is developed in Romans 5 and Philippians 2:8. Hebrews 5:7-9 reflects on Jesus' prayers with loud cries and His learned obedience.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To reveal the depth of Jesus' anguish and the completeness of His submission to the Father's will, to show His death as chosen and Scripture-fulfilling rather than forced, and to expose the disciples' weakness against His faithfulness.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes Jesus' unique redemptive obedience. It prescribes by example: watch and pray against temptation, submit to the Father's will in trial, and trust God rather than the sword. The disciples' failure warns against overconfidence like Peter's.

debates.question: What is the cup Jesus prays might pass from Him?

views.label: The cup of God's wrath against sin
views.case: In the prophets the cup is God's judgment poured out; Jesus' anguish is not mere fear of death but the horror of bearing the wrath due to sinners, drinking the cup of judgment in their place, which explains His agony beyond that of many calm martyrs.

views.label: The suffering and death itself
views.case: Some stress the cup as the whole ordeal of betrayal, torture, and crucifixion, and the desolation of bearing sin; the emphasis falls on the real human dread of the cross, without narrowing the cup to a single idea.

debates.bearing: Both agree Jesus faced something far heavier than ordinary death and embraced it in obedience; understanding the cup as God's judgment against sin most fully accounts for His agony and for the atonement the cross accomplishes.

study.originalLanguages: In Gethsemane Jesus confronts the poterion, the cup, which in the prophets is the cup of God's wrath that the guilty must drink; His sorrow to the point of death is the dread of bearing that judgment for sinners. He asks Peter, James, and John to gregoreo, to keep watch with Him, but they sleep, and the weakness of the flesh is laid bare. Three times He prays, and the whole of His prayer resolves into thelema, the Father's will: not as I will, but as You will; if this cup cannot pass unless I drink it, may Your will be done. Here the Son surrenders His human will to the Father's, and by that obedience He takes up the cup and goes to the cross.

typology.fulfillment: Jesus struck so the scattered sheep might be gathered and restored, Zechariah 13:7, John 10:11.
The judgment against sin that Jesus drinks in the place of His people, Isaiah 51:17, Psalm 75:8.

typology.type: The obedient Son in a garden
typology.fulfillment: The last Adam surrendering His will where the first Adam grasped his own, reversing Eden's fall, Romans 5:19.

study.christ: Jesus is the Shepherd who will be struck so that the scattered sheep may be gathered, and the obedient Son whose agony in the garden reveals the weight of what He came to bear. He faces the cup of God's judgment with real human dread, yet three times surrenders His will to the Father, choosing the cross in love. He is no helpless victim: He could summon more than twelve legions of angels, but He lays down His life so the Scriptures might be fulfilled. Where the first Adam seized his own will in a garden and plunged the world into ruin, the last Adam yields His will in a garden and opens the way of salvation, going willingly to His betrayal and arrest.
study.god: The Father's will, embraced in Gethsemane, is the cross, and the whole scene reveals a God who saves through the surrendered obedience of His Son rather than by sparing Him. God ordains the suffering the prophets foretold, so that even the betrayal, the armed crowd, and the flight of the disciples fulfill the Scriptures and serve His purpose. He is almighty, able in an instant to send legions of angels, yet He withholds rescue because a greater rescue is underway. In answering His Son's prayer not by removing the cup but by giving strength to drink it, God shows that His deepest deliverance comes through, not around, the cross.

crossReferences.note: Strike the Shepherd and the sheep will be scattered, quoted by Jesus about that night.
The cup of God's wrath, the background to the cup Jesus prays over.
Christ humbled Himself and became obedient to death, the obedience embodied in Gethsemane.
Jesus offering prayers with loud cries and tears, and learning obedience through suffering.

study.oneStory: In a garden, where the first man grasped his own will and fell, the last Adam surrenders His will to the Father and takes up the cup of judgment that should have been poured on us. Struck as the Shepherd so the scattered sheep might one day be gathered, Jesus goes willingly to His betrayal and arrest, refusing rescue so the Scriptures may be fulfilled. His disciples flee, and He stands alone. The story presses on to the cross, where the cup is drunk to the dregs, and the obedience of Gethsemane secures the salvation that Eden lost.

quiz.q: What was the heart of Jesus' prayer in Gethsemane?

options.text: Surrender to the Father's will: not as I will, but as You will.
options.note: Verses 39 and 42 center the prayer on submission to the Father's will.

options.text: A request that the cup be taken from Him.
options.note: He did ask this, but always subordinated it to the Father's will, which is the deeper point.

options.text: A demand that God rescue Him from His enemies.
options.note: He refused rescue; He submitted rather than demanding deliverance.

options.text: A prayer that His disciples would stay awake.
options.note: He asked them to watch, but His own prayer was submission to the Father's will.

quiz.q: What does the cup Jesus prayed about most fully represent?

options.text: The cup of God's judgment against sin, which He would drink in the place of sinners.
options.note: The prophetic cup of wrath best explains His agony and the atonement.

options.text: The physical pain of crucifixion He was about to endure.
options.note: It includes His suffering, but the cup carries the deeper sense of bearing God's judgment, which this omits.

options.text: A test of whether He would stay loyal to the disciples.
options.note: The cup concerns His mission to bear judgment, not loyalty to the disciples.

options.text: A symbol of the Passover wine at the Supper.
options.note: This cup is the cup of judgment He must drink, not the Passover cup.

quiz.q: Why did Jesus refuse to be defended by the sword?

options.text: He could summon legions of angels, but chose to fulfill the Scriptures and drink the cup.
options.note: Verses 52-54 show His deliberate choice to let Scripture be fulfilled.

options.text: He wanted to avoid unnecessary violence at His arrest.
options.note: He did rebuke the sword, but the deeper reason was to fulfill the Scriptures, not merely to avoid violence.

options.text: He had no power to resist those who came for Him.
options.note: He explicitly could call twelve legions of angels; His surrender was chosen, not forced.

options.text: The disciples had told Him not to resist.
options.note: A disciple drew a sword to defend Him; Jesus overruled that, choosing the cross.

quiz.q: How does Matthew present Jesus in His arrest?

options.text: As the willing, sovereign Son fulfilling the Scriptures, not a helpless victim.
options.note: His refusal of angels and appeal to Scripture show His willing sovereignty.

options.text: As a righteous man overpowered by His enemies.
options.note: He is seized, but Matthew stresses His choice and control, which this misses.

options.text: As someone caught off guard by the betrayal.
options.note: He foretold the betrayal and met it knowingly, calling Judas friend.

options.text: As a rebel resisting arrest by force.
options.note: He stopped the sword and submitted peacefully.

questions: Jesus told His friends to watch and pray so they would not fall into temptation, yet they slept. Where is prayerful watchfulness the very thing you are neglecting before a testing time?
In agony Jesus still prayed, not as I will, but as You will. What would it look like to pray that honest prayer over the hardest thing you are facing?
Peter was sure he would never fall. Where might your confidence in your own faithfulness actually be a warning sign?
Jesus drank the cup of judgment so you would never have to. How does that reshape the way you face your own suffering, knowing the worst cup has already been drained for you?
Jesus could have called legions of angels but chose the cross for you. How does His willing surrender move your worship and your obedience today?

translationNotes.note: The quotation is Zechariah 13:7, I will strike the Shepherd and the sheep will be scattered; presented here as prose for a clean reading and applied to that very night.
The cup echoes the prophetic cup of God's wrath (Isaiah 51:17, Jeremiah 25:15); Jesus prays for its removal yet submits to the Father, embracing it in the sinner's place.
Friend, do what you came for may also be rendered Friend, for what have you come; either way Jesus meets the betrayal knowingly and with sober composure.
